content.full_text:
Farn PorrrrcAl- PnecrrcEs CovrurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 958t4-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
May 22,2003
Adam U. Lindgren
Meyers, Nave, Riback, Silver & Wilson
555 lzn Street, Suite 1500
Oakland, CA94607
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4.-03-065
Dear Mr. Lindgren:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Half Moon Bay
City Councilmember Deborah Ruddock regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions oi
the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1
QUESTIONS
CONCLUSIONS
l. Since the owner of this real property is still Councilmernber Ruddock,s spouse, and
thus a member of her "immediate family," the council member has an ..ooo*i" interest
in that property under section 82033.
1. Does Councilmember Ruddock have an economic interest in her husband,s real
property interest if she has quitclaimed her interest in the property pursuant to divorce?
2. May Councilmember Ruddock participate in a decision to develop a former railroad
right-of-way located within 500 feet of herhusband,s real property interest?
2' Itis presumed that the financial effect of this decision on the property owned by the
council member's spouse is material and that she has a conflict olinterest in this
decision. As a result, she is prohibited from participating in this decision unless she can
rebut this presumption.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
'l::


File No. 4-03-065
Page No. 2
FACTS
The Half Moon Bay City Council will be asked in the near future to make
decisions tegatdingthe development of a former railroadright-of-way running through
the city. Until recently, Ms. Ruddock and her husband o*nid real prlperty located -
within 500 feet of the right-of-way. Ms. Ruddock and her husband-are now separated
and in the process of obtaining a divorce. As a result of the divorce process, Ms.
Ruddock has quitclaimed her interest in the subject property to her husband. In addition,
Ms. Ruddock informed you that an agreement regarding *re aivision of assets between
them has now been finalized and she does not expect that ttre issue of asset division will
be reopened. Ms. Ruddock and her husband have two children together, one of which is
over 18 years of age. Neither child has an ownership interest in th; subject property.
ANALYSIS
The primary pu{pose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their o*n financial interests or the financial
interests pfpersons who have supported them." (Section 8I001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits apublic official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his orher official position to influence a gJvernmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2
Steps One and Two: Is Deborah Ruddock considered a 66public officialD and is she
making participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the Half Moon Bay city council, Deborah Ruddock is a
"member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency,'and,
therefore, is a public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions olth" A"t.
(Section 82048; regulation 1 S70t (a).)
A public offictal "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matteE obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (see regulation tglo2.l.i
Councilmember Ruddock will '?nake a govemmental decision,, if she votes on a
decision regarding development standards or whether to permit certain subdivision.
Additionally, if she engages in any of the actions described in enclosed regulations
' These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regulation 1g700(b).


File No. 4-03-065
Page No. 3
18702.2 and 18702.3 with regard to this decision, she will "participate in making,, or
"influence" that decision.
Step Three: What is Councilmember Ruddock's economic interest - 
the possible
source of a conflict of interest?
a
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interes t , in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable thatthedecision will have a
mateial financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,on the
official, a member ofhis or her immediate farily," or on any of Ur" olficial's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,trusteg e*ploy"e,
or holds any position of management (section s7103(d); regulation r sio:. i@l);
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or rnore (section szr0:@); regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of incomg including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months priorio the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation I 87 03.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section -
871 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his orher immediate family - this iJthe "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87|03; regulation 13703.5).
"Interest in real propert5z" includes:
a
a
"...arry leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property-located in
the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or tenenci ally by
the public official, or other filer, or his or her immeiiate
family if the fair market value of the interest is two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. Interests in real property
of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in reat
property of any business entity or trust in which the
individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a l}-percent interest or greater.,' (Seciion
82033. Emphasis added.)


File No. 4-03-065
Page No. 4
An indirect investrnent or interest means any investrnent or interest owned by the
spouse of an official or by a member of the official's immediate family,by anagent on
behalf of apublic official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the
official's immediate fanily, or their agents own directly, indirectly, orbeneficially a
1O-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediatefarnlly,, is defined ai section
82029 as an ofEcial's spouse and dependent children.
We have consistently advised that apublic official will continue to have an
indirect interest in real property owned by his or her spouse until such time as dissolution
of marriage proceedings are final. (See Mattas AdviceLetter,No. I-0I-15 2; Mattas
Advice Lettet, No. A-01-290.)r This advice applies even in the situatior, *ir"r" the real
property interest was initially held as separate property rather than community property.
(Mat tas, s upra, l- 0 I - I 52.)
Please note that the Morales letter, sLq)rd, which you cited in your
correspondence, analyzes whether an official has an economic interest in the income of
the official's spouse. The distinction between an economic interest in income, as
opposed to real property, of an official's spouse is important since the statutory definition
of "income" expressly includes "any community projerQt interest in the income of a
spouse." (Section 82030. Emphasis added.) In conhast, section 82033 does not contain
this community property language so that an "interest in real property,'is based on a
spouse's ownership of the real property and not whether the rial property is community
property.
Since the owner of the property located within 500 feet of the railroad right-of-
way is still Councilmember Ruddock's spousg and thus a member of her.tmmediate
family," the council member.has an economic interest in that property under section
82033.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests of
Councilmember Ruddock's. For purposes of this letter, we assume that she has no other
economic interests relevant to the decisions you have identified.
Step Four: fs Councilmember Ruddock's economic interest directly or indirectly
involved in the governmental decision?
Real pr.operty is directly involved in a governmental decision if that real property , .
is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject of the govemmental decision. @egulati on lglo4.z(rxi).)
You have stated that the decision is regarding the future development of the
former railroad right-of-way. As such, this right-of-way is the subject of the decision,
-3 we have upp-li:d l limilar analysis to indirect investonent interests of a spouse. (See Morales
Advice Letter,No. A-99-246(a), footnote 3, citing the RyanAdvice Letter, No. A-gg-027 alld JohnsAdvice
Letter, No. A-92-657.)


File No. ,4.-03-065
Page No. 5
and the property of the council member's spouse will be directly involved in the decision
since it is located within 500 feet of the righfof-way.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the mateiality standards of regulation rclfiS.Z1a)
apply. (Regulation lS7 0a.2@)(t).)
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property which is directly involved in the governmentJ decision is
presumed to be material. "This presumption may be rebuttld by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will havi any financial effect on
the real ptopefir." (Ibid.) Please note that "any financial effect" includes as liffle as a
penny's worth.
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon Councilmember Ruddock's economic interest will meet the applicable
materiality standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered'?easonably foreseeable,, if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. @egulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, Uut ii must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC Ops. 198.)
It is presumed that the financial effect of this decision on the property owned by
the council member's spouse is materjal and that she has a conflict oiioi"rot in this
decision. As a result, she is prohibited from participating in this decision unless she can
rebut this presumption. In order to rebut the presumption of regulation 18705.2(a)(l),
Councilmember Ruddock must show that it is notreasonably foreseeable that the
decision (i.e., the decision to develop the former right-of-way) will have any financial
effect on her spouse's properfy.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination of whether ornot it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for
Councilmember Ruddgckls economic interest is necessarily'a factual question"that is 
. -. ,", .
ultimately for the council member to decide.
steps Seven and Eight: The'6Public Generally, andrrLegally Required
Participation" Exceptions
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "public generally', and
"legally required participation" rules, exceptions to the conflicrof-inierest rdl"., ur.
appli cabl e to Councilmemb er Ruddo ck, s situation.


File No. 4.-03-065
Page No. 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (91 6)
322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: r;
t
Itt
Natalie
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
NBie
